title: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T
authors: Rachel L. Amdur, Jeffrey A. Linder

items.subtopic: Upper Respiratory Symptoms, Including Earache, Sinus Symptoms, and Sore Throat | UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS
items.title: Common Causes and Duration of Upper Respiratory Symptoms
items.content: Upper respiratory symptoms are predominantly viral but can also arise from various other conditions. Common causes include otitis externa, acute otitis media, sinusitis, pharyngitis, and more. Typically, these symptoms last hours to a few days at presentation.
keyPoints: Most common cause: Viral infection
Common causes of ear pain: Otitis externa, AOM, OME, acute mastoiditis
Sinus symptoms caused by: Acute sinusitis, invasive fungal sinusitis, nosocomial sinusitis, chronic sinusitis

Sore throat and neck pain caused by: Streptococcal pharyngitis, nonstreptococcal pharyngitis, infectious mononucleosis, other bacterial pharyngitis, Lemierre syndrome, gonococcal pharyngitis, diphtheria, acute HIV infection, head and neck abscesses, epiglottitis, laryngitis
Duration of symptoms: Hours to a few days
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Upper Respiratory Symptoms, Including Earache, Sinus Symptoms, and Sore Throat | UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: Upper Respiratory Symptoms, Including Earache, Sinus Symptoms, and Sore Throat | UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS
items.title: Prevention and Epidemiology of Upper Respiratory Infections (URIs)
items.content: URIs are the most common reason for seeking care in the U.S., with adults experiencing 2–4 episodes annually, while children may have 6–10. Preventive measures include hand hygiene, physical distancing, mask use, isolation, and environmental cleaning.
keyPoints: Most common symptomatic reason for care: URIs
Annual URI frequency: Adults 2-4 times; Children 6-10 times
Prevention methods: Hand washing/sanitization, physical distancing, facial masks, isolation of ill persons, environmental cleaning
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Upper Respiratory Symptoms, Including Earache, Sinus Symptoms, and Sore Throat | UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS

items.subtopic: IMPROVING AMBULATORY ANTIBIOTIC PRESCRIBING | AMBULATORY ANTIBIOTIC STEWARDSHIP
items.title: Rationale Against Routine Antibiotic Use for Acute Respiratory Infections
items.content: Most acute respiratory infections (ARIs) are caused by viruses and do not benefit from antibiotic treatment. Antibiotics should be reserved for specific bacterial infections such as AOM, sinusitis, streptococcal pharyngitis, and pneumonia, where only a minority of cases warrant antibiotics.
keyPoints: Antibiotics are inappropriate for the majority of ARIs due to their viral etiology.
Common respiratory viruses cause most ARIs which are self-limited.
At least half of ambulatory antibiotic prescriptions in the U.S. are deemed inappropriate.
Inappropriate use leads to adverse effects, altered microbiome, C. difficile infection, increased costs, and antibiotic resistance.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · IMPROVING AMBULATORY ANTIBIOTIC PRESCRIBING | AMBULATORY ANTIBIOTIC STEWARDSHIP

items.title: Core Elements of Outpatient Antibiotic Stewardship
items.content: The CDC's Core Elements for outpatient antibiotic stewardship emphasize improving prescribing practices through commitment, policy implementation, monitoring, and education. Effective strategies include peer comparison, clinical decision support, patient education, and communication training.
keyPoints: Ambulatory use accounts for ~85% of antibiotics in developed countries.
CDC's Core Elements: commit to improvement, implement policies, monitor prescribing, provide educational resources.
Effective interventions: peer comparison, accountable justification, precommitment, clinical decision support, patient education.
Communication training is effective when it includes clear diagnosis and guidance on illness course and red flags.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · IMPROVING AMBULATORY ANTIBIOTIC PRESCRIBING | AMBULATORY ANTIBIOTIC STEWARDSHIP

items.subtopic: DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | SYMPTOMS AND SIGNS
items.title: Understanding Nonspecific URI Etiology and Common Viral Causes
items.content: Nonspecific upper respiratory infections (URIs) are primarily caused by a variety of respiratory viruses, with rhinoviruses being the most common. Despite the availability of multiplex PCR panels for viral detection, specific diagnosis is generally unnecessary due to cost-effectiveness concerns and potential false positives from recent infections.
keyPoints: Rhinoviruses are the most common cause, with over 100 serotypes.
Other common viruses include coronavirus, parainfluenza virus, respiratory syncytial virus, influenza virus, adenovirus (57 serotypes), metapneumovirus, and bocavirus.
Specific viral diagnosis is not practical or cost-effective; consider multiplex PCR panels with caution due to potential false positives.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | SYMPTOMS AND SIGNS

items.subtopic: DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | SYMPTOMS AND SIGNS
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Course of Nonspecific URI
items.content: Nonspecific URIs typically present with a gradual onset of symptoms such as nasal fullness, rhinorrhea, sore throat, and low-grade fever. Symptoms usually persist for 5–10 days but can last up to 14 days. Physical examination may reveal conjunctivitis, pharyngeal erythema, or nasal mucosa changes.
keyPoints: Incubation period ranges from 2–8 days.
Symptoms include nasal fullness, rhinorrhea, sore throat, laryngitis, lymphadenopathy, cough, and low-grade fever.
Myalgias are less prominent compared to influenza.
Epistaxis can occur with frequent nose blowing.
Physical findings may include conjunctivitis, pharyngeal erythema, exudates, or cobblestoning.
Nasal mucosa changes and lung sounds (clear, wheezing, bronchial) are possible.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | SYMPTOMS AND SIGNS

items.title: Management of Nonspecific URI in Adults and Older Children
items.content: Treatment for nonspecific upper respiratory infections (URI) in adults and older children is symptom-based. Acetaminophen or NSAIDs like ibuprofen can be used to manage fever, myalgias, and sore throat. Rhinorrhea may be treated with ipratropium bromide, while nasal congestion can be managed with oxymetazoline (two sprays into each nostril twice a day for up to 5 days) or systemic decongestants like pseudoephedrine. Combination products that include decongestants with analgesics or antihistamines may also help relieve symptoms.
keyPoints: Symptom-based treatment is recommended for adults and older children.
Acetaminophen or NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) can be used for fever, myalgias, and sore throat.
Rhinorrhea can be treated with ipratropium bromide.
Nasal congestion managed with oxymetazoline: two sprays into each nostril twice a day for up to 5 days or systemic decongestants like pseudoephedrine.
Combination products may help relieve symptoms.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Nonspecific Upper Respiratory Infection

items.title: Red Flags and Management in Children <6 Years Old
items.content: For children under 6 years old, cough and cold medicines should not be used due to the risk of adverse effects. Honey can soothe a sore throat for children over 1 year old, while cool-mist humidifiers may aid breathing. Saline nasal drops and bulb suctioning are recommended for nasal congestion. Parents should be informed that symptoms typically peak early but can last up to 14 days; patients remain infectious as long as they have symptoms. Red flags warranting medical attention include fever >102°F, chest pain (not from a pulled muscle), shortness of breath, dizziness, confusion, new ear or sinus pain, and symptoms lasting more than 14 days.
keyPoints: Cough and cold medicines should not be used in children <6 years old due to adverse effects.
Honey can soothe sore throats for children >1 year old.
Cool-mist humidifiers may help with breathing; saline nasal drops and bulb suctioning for congestion.
Inform parents that symptoms peak early but can last up to 14 days; patients are infectious as long as symptomatic.
Red flags include fever >102°F, chest pain (not from pulled muscle), shortness of breath, dizziness, confusion, new ear or sinus pain, and symptoms lasting >14 days.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Nonspecific Upper Respiratory Infection

items.title: Consider Temporal Arteritis in Older Adults with Ear Pain and Systemic Symptoms
items.content: In adults over 50 years old presenting with ear pain, headache, malaise, weight loss, fever, anorexia, and a normal ear exam, temporal arteritis should be considered. This condition requires prompt diagnosis to prevent complications such as vision loss.
keyPoints: Temporal arteritis consideration in patients >50 years with systemic symptoms
Symptoms include headache, malaise, weight loss, fever, anorexia
Normal ear exam despite symptoms
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · EAR PAIN

items.title: Identify Malignant Otitis Externa in Immunocompromised Patients with Severe Ear Pain
items.content: Malignant otitis externa is a severe, potentially life-threatening condition involving the temporal bone, primarily affecting immunocompromised patients such as those with diabetes. It can lead to cranial nerve involvement and systemic symptoms like fever.
keyPoints: Occurs in immunocompromised individuals, often with diabetes
Involves the temporal bone
Can affect cranial nerves VII, IX, XI, or XII
Associated with fever
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · EAR PAIN

items.title: Management of Otitis Externa and Indications for Escalation
items.content: Topical antibacterial drugs with glucocorticoids are the mainstay treatment for otitis externa, lasting 7–10 days. If no improvement occurs within 2-4 days, ear wicks or gauze impregnated with anti-infective agents can be used. Oral antibiotics and topical antifungals are ineffective. For malignant otitis externa, oral antipseudomonal antibiotics may be necessary.
keyPoints: Topical treatment for 7–10 days is standard; use ciprofloxacin-hydrocortisone as an alternative to polymyxin B-neomycin-hydrocortisone due to ototoxicity risk with tympanic membrane perforation.
If no improvement within 2-4 days, consider ear wicks or gauze impregnated with anti-infective agents.
Oral antibiotics and topical antifungals are ineffective for otitis externa.
Malignant otitis externa may require oral antipseudomonal antibiotics.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Otitis Externa | ACUTE OTITIS MEDIA

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria and Epidemiology of Acute Otitis Media (AOM)
items.content: Acute otitis media predominantly affects children aged 6–24 months, with ~60% experiencing an episode by age 6. It is caused by viral URIs leading to bacterial infection. Diagnosis requires moderate to severe tympanic membrane bulging or new-onset otorrhea not due to otitis externa. Mild bulging can be diagnosed if symptoms persist <48 hours or intense erythema is present.
keyPoints: AOM incidence peaks at 6–24 months; ~60% of children experience an episode by age 6.
Caused by viral URIs leading to bacterial infection, commonly Streptococcus pneumoniae, nontypeable Haemophilus influenzae, and Moraxella catarrhalis.
Diagnosis requires moderate to severe tympanic membrane bulging or new-onset otorrhea; mild bulging can be diagnosed with symptoms <48 hours or intense erythema.
Conduct hearing loss is present in AOM patients.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Otitis Externa | ACUTE OTITIS MEDIA

items.title: When to Prescribe Antibiotics for AOM in Children
items.content: Antibiotic treatment is indicated for children with specific risk factors or severe symptoms. The decision should consider the modest benefits versus potential adverse effects.
keyPoints: Indications for antibiotics: age <6 months, bilateral ear findings (children 6 months to 2 years), otorrhea (>6 months old), severe otalgia, pain >48 hours, or fever >102.2°F.
Number needed to treat (NNT) for decreased pain by day 2 or 3 is 20.
Number needed to harm (NNH) for vomiting, diarrhea, or rash with antibiotics is 14.
Severe complications like mastoiditis are rare; NNT to prevent one case of mastoiditis is ~5000.
American Academy of Family Physicians recommends against routine antibiotic use in children 2–12 years old with nonsevere symptoms.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Acute Otitis Media

items.title: Management and Follow-Up for AOM in Children
items.content: Antibiotic choice and duration depend on age and severity. If no improvement after 48–72 hours, change the antibiotic regimen.
keyPoints: Preferred antibiotic: high-dose amoxicillin (90 mg/kg per day, up to 3 g). Alternatives include cefdinir, cefuroxime, cefpodoxime, or IM ceftriaxone.
If previous amoxicillin use in past 30 days, prescribe amoxicillin/clavulanate (90/6.4 mg/kg per day) in two divided doses.
Duration: 10 days for children <2 years old or severe symptoms; 5–7 days for children 2–5 years with mild to moderate AOM; 5 days for children ≥6 years with mild/moderate symptoms.
Change antibiotic if no improvement after 48–72 hours to amoxicillin/clavulanate, second- or third-generation cephalosporin, or IM ceftriaxone for 3 days.
Refer to specialist if condition does not improve after changing antibiotics.
Middle-ear effusions resolve over 3 months; consider tympanostomy tubes for recurrent AOM (three episodes in 6 months or four episodes in 1 year).
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Acute Otitis Media

items.subtopic: OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion
items.title: OME: Common in Young Children and Often Resolves Spontaneously
items.content: Otitis media with effusion (OME) is characterized by fluid accumulation in the middle ear without acute infection, predominantly affecting children under 2 years old. It often follows a viral upper respiratory infection or can be related to allergies, craniofacial abnormalities, gastroesophageal reflux, and enlarged adenoids.
keyPoints: Most common in children <2 years old (>60% of cases).
Symptoms include decreased sound conduction and hearing, with potential language development issues.
Typical examination findings: translucent or gray tympanic membrane with fluid, air-fluid levels, bubbles; loss of light reflex; reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy.
Resolves spontaneously within 4–6 weeks. Chronic if persists >3 months.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.subtopic: OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion
items.title: Management of OME: Myringotomy and Tympanostomy Tubes
items.content: OME management primarily involves myringotomy with tympanostomy tube insertion, especially for persistent cases. Adenoidectomy may be considered in young children with nasal obstruction or recurrent infections. Medications like antihistamines, glucocorticoids, or antibiotics are not reliably effective.
keyPoints: Myringotomy with tympanostomy tube insertion is the primary treatment.
Adenoidectomy may benefit those with nasal obstruction or recurrent OME.
Medications do not reliably improve outcomes.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.title: Acute Mastoiditis: Serious Infection in Young Children
items.content: Acute mastoiditis is a severe infection often following acute otitis media, primarily affecting children under 2 years old. It involves the spread of organisms from the middle ear to the mastoid air cells and can lead to complications like subperiosteal abscesses.
keyPoints: Most common in children <2 years old.
Complications include facial nerve palsy, labyrinthitis, skull osteomyelitis, and more severe intracranial infections.
Laboratory findings: elevated inflammatory markers, white blood cells with neutrophilia.
Imaging (CT or MRI) may be needed for suspected complications.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.title: Management of Mastoiditis: Indications for Hospital Admission and Surgical Intervention
items.content: Patients presenting with mastoiditis require hospital admission and initiation of IV antibiotic therapy along with myringotomy, potentially accompanied by tympanostomy tubes. If there is no improvement within 48 hours, a mastoidectomy should be considered.
keyPoints: Admit patients with mastoiditis for IV antibiotics and myringotomy/tympanostomy tubes.
Perform mastoidectomy if no improvement in 48 hours.
Send tympanostomy/myringotomy samples or subperiosteal abscess drainage for culture and sensitivity testing.
Empirical IV antibiotic therapy: vancomycin (concern about resistant S. pneumoniae or MRSA) or a cephalosporin (e.g., cefepime, ceftazidime).
For recurrent AOM or recent antibiotics, use vancomycin plus an antipseudomonal penicillin.
Continue IV antibiotic therapy for 7–10 days and complete a 4-week course of oral antibiotics.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Acute Sinusitis: Antibiotic Use Criteria
items.content: Acute sinusitis is often viral, leading to unnecessary antibiotic prescriptions. Only a small percentage (<2%) of cases are bacterial, yet antibiotics are prescribed in over 70% of visits for sinusitis.
keyPoints: Majority of acute sinusitis cases are caused by respiratory viruses.
<2% of sinusitis episodes are due to bacteria (most often S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, or M. catarrhalis).
>70% of office visits for sinusitis result in antibiotic prescriptions.
Guideline criteria suggest no more than 50% of adults—and probably closer to 20%—meet the criteria for antibiotic prescribing.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.title: Recognizing and Responding to Complications in Sinusitis: Emergency Indicators
items.content: Complications from sinusitis are rare but can be severe, including orbital cellulitis, osteomyelitis, meningitis, intracranial abscesses, and cavernous sinus thrombosis. New symptoms such as confusion, unilateral weakness, proptosis, limited ocular movements, or acute vision changes warrant immediate evaluation.
keyPoints: Complications from sinusitis are rare but can include orbital cellulitis, osteomyelitis, meningitis, intracranial abscesses, and cavernous sinus thrombosis.
New symptoms indicating complications: confusion, unilateral weakness, proptosis, limited ocular movements, acute vision changes.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.title: When to Prescribe Antibiotics for Acute Sinusitis
items.content: Antibiotic therapy should be considered for patients with acute sinusitis who exhibit persistent, severe, or worsening symptoms after initial treatment with decongestants and analgesics for 2–4 days. The first-line antibiotic is amoxicillin/clavulanate (875 mg/125 mg bid for 7 days), with amoxicillin (875 mg PO bid for 7 days) as an alternative. For patients with mild penicillin allergies, cefuroxime can be used, while doxycycline is suitable for those with severe penicillin allergies.
keyPoints: Antibiotics are indicated for persistent, severe, or worsening symptoms after 2–4 days of decongestants and analgesics.
First-line antibiotic: amoxicillin/clavulanate (875 mg/125 mg bid for 7 days).
Alternative antibiotics include amoxicillin (875 mg PO bid for 7 days), cefuroxime, and doxycycline.
Macrolides are not recommended due to high rates of macrolide-resistant S. pneumoniae.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Acute Sinusitis

items.title: Red Flags and Further Management in Acute Sinusitis
items.content: Patients should be advised to reconsult if they experience recurrent fever >102°F, worsening sinus symptoms after initial improvement, or rapid worsening of persistent facial pain. If there is no improvement after 3–5 days of antibiotic therapy, consider second-line treatments like amoxicillin/clavulanate (2000 mg/125 mg bid for 7 days) or levofloxacin. Referral to an otorhinolaryngologist and imaging with noncontrast CT may be necessary if symptoms persist or complications are suspected.
keyPoints: Red flags include recurrent fever >102°F, worsening sinus symptoms after initial improvement, and rapid worsening of facial pain.
Re-evaluate patients without improvement after 3–5 days of antibiotic therapy.
Second-line antibiotics: amoxicillin/clavulanate (2000 mg/125 mg bid for 7 days) or levofloxacin.
Consider referral to an otorhinolaryngologist and noncontrast CT imaging if symptoms persist.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Acute Sinusitis

items.title: Understanding Chronic Sinusitis Types and Their Characteristics
items.content: Chronic sinusitis can be categorized into three main types based on the presence of polyps and associated conditions. These are: (1) chronic sinusitis without polyps, which is more common among women and often presents with facial pain; (2) chronic sinusitis with polyps, more prevalent in men and characterized by anosmia or asthma; and (3) allergic fungal sinusitis, typically occurring in younger adults from humid regions, associated with IgE-mediated allergy.
keyPoints: Chronic sinusitis lasts >12 weeks.
Types: without polyps, with polyps, allergic fungal rhinosinusitis.
Without polyps: more common in women, facial pain, $T_{H}$ lymphocyte predominance.
With polyps: more common in men, anosmia/asthma, $T_{H}^{2}$ lymphocyte predominance.
Allergic fungal sinusitis: young adults, warm regions, IgE-mediated allergy.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · CHRONIC SINUSITIS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Red Flags in Chronic Sinusitis
items.content: Diagnosis of chronic sinusitis requires confirmation through anterior rhinoscopy, nasal endoscopy, or imaging due to the possibility that up to 40% of patients with symptoms may not show mucosal changes. Noncontrast CT is preferred for imaging. Unilateral symptoms or polyps, especially with bloody discharge, should raise concern about potential tumors.
keyPoints: Diagnosis confirmed by anterior rhinoscopy, nasal endoscopy, or imaging.
Up to 40% of patients may not show mucosal changes despite symptoms.
Noncontrast CT is the imaging modality of choice.
Unilateral symptoms/polyps with bloody discharge suggest possible tumors.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · CHRONIC SINUSITIS

items.subtopic: Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN
items.title: Management Strategies for Chronic Sinusitis
items.content: For chronic sinusitis, avoidance of triggers and use of saline sprays are initial steps. Intranasal glucocorticoids like mometasone and fluticasone are central to treatment, particularly in cases with polyps. Oral glucocorticoids may be used for 2–3 weeks if intranasal steroids fail, especially in patients with polyps. Antibiotics have limited evidence of efficacy; however, macrolides might benefit those without polyps over a 3-month period.
keyPoints: Intranasal glucocorticoids are mainstays for chronic sinusitis with polyps.
Oral glucocorticoids can be effective for 2–3 weeks in unresponsive cases, especially with polyps.
Antibiotics have little evidence of effectiveness; macrolides may help those without polyps over 3 months.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN

items.subtopic: Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAY
items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Management of Sore Throat
items.content: Sore throat can result from various causes beyond pharyngitis, including abscesses, thyroiditis, reflux, tumors, and postnasal drainage. Acute pharyngitis is often viral but can be bacterial (e.g., GAS). Pharyngeal erythema typically indicates a viral infection, while exudate should not be confused with Candida or leukoplakia.
keyPoints: Sore throat can be caused by various conditions beyond pharyngitis.
Acute pharyngitis is often due to viruses but can also involve GAS and other pathogens.
Pharyngeal erythema usually suggests a viral infection.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN

items.title: Importance of Identifying GAS Pharyngitis
items.content: Prompt antibiotic treatment for GAS pharyngitis in adults can reduce symptoms, prevent disease spread, and decrease suppurative complications.
keyPoints: GAS is the only common cause of sore throat requiring antibiotics.
About 10% of adults with sore throats have GAS infection.
Antibiotic treatment reduces suppurative complications like peritonsillar abscess.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.title: Centor Criteria for Diagnosing Streptococcal Pharyngitis
items.content: The Centor criteria help stratify the probability of GAS infection in adults, guiding testing and treatment decisions.
keyPoints: Centor criteria include: fever history, absence of cough, tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy, tonsillar exudate/swelling.
Probability of GAS infection is 2% with no points and 41% with four points.
Area under the curve for Centor criteria is 0.79.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.title: Guidelines for Rapid Strep Testing and Treatment
items.content: Rapid antigen tests guide treatment decisions, with specific recommendations based on Centor criteria scores.
keyPoints: Rapid GAS-specific antigen tests have ~80% sensitivity and ~95% specificity.
Test/no treatment threshold is set at 5%; adults meeting no or one criterion should not be tested or treated.
Adults with two or three Centor criteria should undergo rapid testing to guide antibiotic use.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.title: Throat Cultures: Sensitivity and Specificity in Diagnosing GAS Infection
items.content: Throat cultures have a sensitivity of ~85–90% for detecting Group A Streptococcus (GAS), but specificity is low (50–70%) due to high carrier rates. Throat cultures are not recommended for routine evaluation of adults with sore throat because the delay in results outweighs the modest gain in sensitivity over rapid testing.
keyPoints: Throat culture sensitivity: ~85–90%
Specificity may be as low as 50–70% among adults and children
Carrier rates: up to 20% in schoolchildren, ~5% in adolescents/young adults
Throat cultures not recommended for routine adult sore throat evaluation due to delay in results
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 2) | Streptococcal Pharyngitis

items.title: Antibiotic Management and Complications of Streptococcal Pharyngitis
items.content: Penicillin is the preferred antibiotic for streptococcal pharyngitis due to its narrow-spectrum, low cost, and lack of resistance. Alternatives include amoxicillin for children, cephalexin or cefadroxil for mild penicillin allergy, and erythromycin, clarithromycin, or clindamycin for severe allergies. Complications are rare but can include acute rheumatic fever and poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
keyPoints: Penicillin is the antibiotic of choice
Amoxicillin is an alternative for children
Cephalexin, cefadroxil for mild penicillin allergy
Erythromycin, clarithromycin, clindamycin for severe penicillin allergy
Complications include acute rheumatic fever and poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 2) | Streptococcal Pharyngitis

items.title: EBV Infection and Mononucleosis in Young Adults
items.content: EBV is a common cause of pharyngitis in young adults, accounting for 1–6% of cases. The full-blown syndrome occurs in about one-fourth of patients with infectious mononucleosis.
keyPoints: EBV rarely causes pharyngitis in adults >40 years
Full-blown acute syndrome includes malaise, fever, sore throat, marked adenopathy
Physical exam often shows marked adenopathy and splenomegaly
Hematologic findings: lymphocyte count >4000/μL or >50% with atypical features in >10%
Serologic finding: heterophil antibody detectable in 40% during first week, 80–90% by third week
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS

items.title: Lemierre Syndrome Considerations
items.content: Consider Lemierre syndrome in patients with non-GAS pharyngitis that is not resolving, especially if accompanied by rigors, neck pain, or swelling.
keyPoints: Caused primarily by Fusobacterium necrophorum
Occurs predominantly in male patients
Characterized by septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein and metastatic infections
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS

items.title: Gonococcal Pharyngitis in Persistent Sore Throat Cases
items.content: Suspect gonococcal pharyngitis in patients with persistent sore throat unresponsive to treatment for presumptive streptococcal pharyngitis.
keyPoints: N. gonorrhoeae causes pharyngitis in 1% of adult primary care sore throat cases
More common in men who have sex with men, women practicing fellatio with a man with genital gonorrhea
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS

items.subtopic: HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Peritonsillar Abscess
items.content: Peritonsillar abscesses, often resulting from bacterial pharyngitis, present with distinct symptoms such as fever, dysphagia, profound throat pain, trismus, and a 'hot potato voice.' These abscesses are typically unilateral and caused by bacteria like viridans group streptococci and F. necrophorum.
keyPoints: Common in male patients
Caused by viridans group streptococci, β-hemolytic streptococci, F. necrophorum, S. aureus, Prevotella, and Bacteroides
Symptoms: fever, dysphagia, profound throat pain, trismus, 'hot potato voice'
Unilateral palate bulging with uvular deviation
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses

items.subtopic: HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses
items.title: Imaging and Treatment Protocols for Suspected Head and Neck Abscesses
items.content: For suspected head and neck abscesses, CT imaging is recommended except in obvious peritonsillar cases. Management includes securing the airway, surgical drainage, and IV antibiotics targeting streptococci, anaerobes, and possibly S. aureus.
keyPoints: CT imaging indicated for suspected head and neck abscesses
Airway management may require fiberoptic intubation or tracheotomy
Surgical drainage: needle aspiration/tonsillectomy for peritonsillar, incision and drainage for others
IV antibiotics: ampicillin/sulbactam, clindamycin plus ceftriaxone, meropenem; penicillin may suffice with adequate source control
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Epiglottitis in Adults
items.content: Epiglottitis presents with sore throat, dysphagia, odynophagia, hoarseness, stridor, or tachypnea. The inflamed epiglottis can protrude into the oropharynx, causing patients to extend their neck and drool. Examination should be cautious due to risk of laryngospasm; hospital observation is crucial due to potential airway obstruction. Imaging with a lateral neck radiograph showing the 'thumb sign' can aid diagnosis but isn't necessary.
keyPoints: Symptoms: sore throat, dysphagia, odynophagia, hoarseness, stridor, tachypnea
Physical signs: inflamed epiglottis protruding into the oropharynx, 'hot potato voice'
Examination risks: laryngospasm from posterior pharynx examination/swabbing
Management: hospital observation, possible intubation/tracheostomy, humidification, glucocorticoids, IV antibiotics, nebulized epinephrine
'Thumb sign' on lateral neck radiograph indicates epiglottis swelling
Common pathogens in adults: S. pneumoniae, S. pyogenes, S. aureus; H. influenza less common due to vaccination
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · EPIGLOTTITIS | LARYNGITIS

items.title: Understanding Causes and Management of Laryngitis
items.content: Laryngitis, inflammation of the larynx, is usually viral in origin from URIs. In children, parainfluenza can cause croup with a 'barking' cough. Bacterial laryngitis may follow viral infection after about 7 days and involves S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis. Fungal laryngitis is rare but possible in immunosuppressed patients or those recently on antibacterials.
keyPoints: Common cause: viral URIs
In children: parainfluenza virus causing croup with 'barking' cough
Bacterial laryngitis can follow viral infection, typically 7 days in
Common bacteria: S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis
Fungal laryngitis consideration: immunosuppressed patients or recent antibacterial treatment
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · EPIGLOTTITIS | LARYNGITIS

items.question: Why are throat cultures not recommended for the routine evaluation of adults with sore throats?
options: They have high specificity but low sensitivity.
The delay in results outweighs their modest gain in sensitivity over rapid testing.
They are too expensive compared to other tests.
They can only detect viral infections.
items.explanation: Throat cultures are not recommended due to the 24- to 48-h delay in test results, which outweighs their modest gain in sensitivity over rapid testing.

items.statement: OME usually resolves spontaneously within 4–6 weeks.
items.explanation: OME typically resolves on its own within this timeframe.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.subtopic: OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS
items.statement: Acute mastoiditis is most common in adults over 40 years old.
items.explanation: Acute mastoiditis is most common among children under 2 years old.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.statement: Sinus transillumination is accurate in diagnosing sinusitis.
items.explanation: Sinus transillumination is not accurate for diagnosing sinusitis.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.statement: Invasive fungal sinusitis should be considered an emergency in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Invasive fungal sinusitis is a medical emergency, especially in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.statement: Patients with mild penicillin allergies can be treated with cefuroxime for acute sinusitis.
items.explanation: Cefuroxime is a reasonable choice for patients with mild penicillin allergies.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Acute Sinusitis

items.statement: Macrolides are the first-line treatment for acute sinusitis.
items.explanation: Macrolides are not recommended due to high rates of macrolide-resistant S. pneumoniae.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Acute Sinusitis

items.statement: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis is typically resistant to medical treatments.
items.explanation: Allergic fungal rhinosinusitis is indeed resistant to medical treatments.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · CHRONIC SINUSITIS

items.statement: Chronic sinusitis without polyps is more common among men.
items.explanation: Chronic sinusitis without polyps is more common among women.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · CHRONIC SINUSITIS

items.subtopic: Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN
items.statement: Decongestants are recommended for long-term use in chronic sinusitis.
items.explanation: Decongestants should be used only sparingly and briefly.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN

items.subtopic: Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN
items.statement: Endoscopic sinus surgery is indicated for patients who have had inadequate responses to medical therapy.
items.explanation: Endoscopic sinus surgery improves quality of life in patients with inadequate responses to medical therapy.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Chronic Sinusitis | SORE THROAT AND NECK PAIN

items.statement: Nonsuppurative complications are common in GAS pharyngitis.
items.explanation: Nonsuppurative complications are rare.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Antibiotic treatment prevents poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
items.explanation: Antibiotic treatment does not prevent poststreptococcal glomerulonephritis.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.statement: Rapid strep tests and throat cultures can differentiate between true infection and GAS carriage.
items.explanation: Rapid strep tests and culture do not differentiate between patients who have true infection and those who are carriers of GAS.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 2) | Streptococcal Pharyngitis

items.statement: Glucocorticoids are recommended as an adjunctive treatment for sore throat and strep throat.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids have been poorly studied and are not recommended due to potential adverse effects.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 2) | Streptococcal Pharyngitis

items.statement: Antibiotic treatment is indicated for nonstreptococcal pharyngitis caused by Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
items.explanation: Antibiotic treatment is not routinely indicated unless group C or G streptococci are identified.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS

items.statement: Diphtheria typically presents with severe pharyngitis beneath a grayish pseudomembrane.
items.explanation: Diphtheria is characterized by mild pharyngitis beneath its characteristic grayish pseudomembrane.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS

items.subtopic: HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses
items.statement: Retropharyngeal abscesses often present after an antecedent URI in children.
items.explanation: Retropharyngeal abscesses can follow an upper respiratory infection (URI) in children.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses

items.subtopic: HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses
items.statement: Patients with head and neck abscesses should always undergo MRI for diagnosis.
items.explanation: CT is recommended for imaging in suspected head and neck abscesses, except possibly for obvious peritonsillar abscesses.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · HEAD AND NECK ABSCESSES | Head and Neck Abscesses

items.statement: A lateral neck radiograph showing the 'thumb sign' is necessary for diagnosing epiglottitis.
items.explanation: The 'thumb sign' on a lateral neck radiograph can demonstrate epiglottis swelling but is not necessary for diagnosis.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · EPIGLOTTITIS | LARYNGITIS

items.statement: Viral URIs are the most common cause of laryngitis.
items.explanation: Laryngitis is most commonly caused by viral upper respiratory infections (URIs).
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · EPIGLOTTITIS | LARYNGITIS

items.subtopic: Upper Respiratory Symptoms, Including Earache, Sinus Symptoms, and Sore Throat | UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS
items.assertion: Upper respiratory infections (URIs) are the most common reason for seeking care in the United States.
items.reason: URIs include conditions like otitis media, sinusitis, and pharyngitis which are prevalent.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why URIs are a common reason for seeking care.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Upper Respiratory Symptoms, Including Earache, Sinus Symptoms, and Sore Throat | UPPER RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS

items.assertion: Patients with nonspecific upper respiratory infection should be informed that symptoms generally peak early.
items.reason: Symptoms can last for up to 14 days, and patients are infectious as long as they have symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why informing about symptom peaking is important.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Nonspecific Upper Respiratory Infection

items.subtopic: OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS
items.assertion: OME can be caused by allergies.
items.reason: Allergies are a predisposing factor for OME.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.subtopic: OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS
items.assertion: Antibiotics reliably treat otitis media with effusion (OME).
items.reason: OME is often a sequela of viral infections.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because antibiotics do not reliably help OME, but the reason is true as OME can follow viral infections.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · OTITIS MEDIA WITH EFFUSION | Otitis Media with Effusion | ACUTE MASTOIDITIS

items.assertion: Antibiotics are prescribed for more than 70% of sinusitis office visits.
items.reason: Most cases of acute sinusitis are caused by respiratory viruses, not bacteria.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the high prescription rate is despite most cases being viral.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.assertion: Nosocomial sinusitis occurs primarily in patients with allergies.
items.reason: Nosocomial sinusitis is suspected when hospitalized patients have fever without another identifiable cause.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; nosocomial sinusitis occurs mainly in critically ill, nasotracheally intubated patients.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · Mastoiditis | SINUS SYMPTOMS | ACUTE SINUSITIS

items.assertion: The Centor criteria can accurately stratify adult patients with suspected streptococcal pharyngitis.
items.reason: Patients with no points have a 2% probability of being infected with GAS, whereas those with 4 points have a probability of 41%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Rapid antigen tests should be performed for all adults with sore throat.
items.reason: The Centor criteria help determine when rapid antigen tests are necessary.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; not all adults need a rapid antigen test, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · STREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS (part 1)

items.assertion: Acute HIV infection should be considered in patients with sore throat and associated systemic symptoms.
items.reason: Only ~1% of patients with viral or mononucleosis-like symptoms have acute HIV infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the low prevalence does not negate considering it clinically.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS

items.assertion: Lemierre syndrome is most common in female patients.
items.reason: It occurs predominantly in male patients.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Lemierre syndrome occurs predominantly in males.
items.reference: Upper Respiratory Symptoms Including Earache Sinus Symptoms and Sore T · NONSTREPTOCOCCAL PHARYNGITIS
